metadata.title: The $0.001 Token: What Happens When Reasoning Costs Almost Nothing

beats.narration_text: Salaam — this is Liam, in for Bear. Based on the essay 'The $0.001 Token: When Machine Intelligence Becomes a Persistent Infrastructure Cost' by Aditi Shinde, for NortheasternISE / Humanitarians AI. What changes when AI reasoning costs almost nothing? In 2021, running a trillion-parameter model for one day cost one million dollars. In 2026: ten thousand. The 2028 projection: one hundred. When the cost of one more token of thinking approaches zero, the incentive structure changes entirely.

props.segment: The $0.001 Token: What Happens When Reasoning Costs Almost Nothing
props.command: What changes when AI reasoning costs almost nothing?
props.runningText: reading AI compute cost curve and economics…
output: Bottleneck was never the algorithm — it was moving the key-value cache.
At $0.001/token: stop rationing AI, start embedding it continuously.

beats.narration_text: Plot the cost per token of AI inference over time. The curve falls 100x over three years. There is an inflection point — a moment where the curve stops being steep and starts being flat and very close to zero. That inflection point is not a projection; it already happened. Before it: AI is a service you invoke carefully. Each call costs money; you design systems to minimize calls. After it: AI is a utility you leave running. Each call costs a fraction of a cent; you design systems to run it continuously. The architectural implications are different on each side of the inflection point.

manim.description: Cost-per-token curve: x-axis is years (2021-2028), y-axis is log-scale cost. Curve falls steeply, inflection point marked with vertical dashed line labeled '~2024-2025'. Left of inflection: 'invoke carefully'. Right of inflection: 'embed continuously'. 100x drop annotated.

beats.narration_text: The bottleneck was never the algorithm. It was the memory wall: moving terabytes of key-value cache between memory and compute. During transformer inference, each token generation requires reading a large key-value cache — the compressed representation of all previous tokens in the context. That cache is too large to keep near the compute. It lives in high-bandwidth memory. Every token generation requires a memory round trip. The ratio of compute time to memory-movement time was inverted — the processor spent more time waiting for memory than computing. New hardware architectures close that gap: processing-in-memory, high-bandwidth stacking, new interconnects. The algorithm was fine. The bus was the problem.

animated_deck.description: Side-by-side timeline: 'before new hardware' — compute bar short, memory-movement bar tall (processor waiting). 'After new hardware' — compute bar tall, memory-movement bar short. Labeled: 'memory wall closed'. Key-value cache labeled as the data being moved.
animated_deck.sparkLine: The algorithm was fine. The bus was the problem.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. In 2021, running a trillion-parameter model for a day cost a million dollars. In 2026: ten thousand. The projected 2028 number: a hundred. The bottleneck was never the algorithm — it was moving terabytes of key-value cache between memory and compute. At a fraction of a cent per token, you stop rationing AI and start embedding it continuously. The world changes not when the algorithm improves, but when the same algorithm runs at a different price point.

props.artifactTitle: The $0.001 Token: What Happens When Reasoning Costs Almost Nothing
artifactLines: Bottleneck was never the algorithm — it was the memory wall (KV cache movement).
At $0.001/token: stop rationing AI, start embedding it continuously.
Same algorithm. Different economics. Different world.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here is the prompt — read it with me. 'Calculate the daily cost of running an AI assistant continuously on a representative workload at 2023 prices versus 2026 prices. At what price point does continuous AI become economically equivalent to hiring a full-time analyst?' That breakeven calculation is the architectural decision. When AI drops below the equivalent annual cost of a human doing the same task, the system design changes. Find that number for your domain. Liam, in for Bear.
